{"step_by_step_thinking": "The patient is a one-day-old male with bilious vomiting, abdominal distention, failure to pass meconium within the first 24-48 hours, and hypoactive bowel sounds—key signs of a bowel obstruction. The mother had gestational diabetes, and the prenatal quadruple screen showed decreased AFP, increased beta-hCG, decreased unconjugated estriol, and increased inhibin A—findings consistent with trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) or other chromosomal abnormalities. However, the most relevant clue is the amniotic fluid index (AFI) of 28 cm, which indicates polyhydramnios. Polyhydramnios is a known feature of both duodenal atresia and meconium ileus. Among the listed conditions, duodenal atresia is associated with polyhydramnios and failure to pass meconium, and it presents with bilious vomiting due to obstruction of the duodenum. Meconium ileus, which is associated with cystic fibrosis, also presents with bilious vomiting and failure to pass meconium, but it is typically associated with a hyperechoic bowel on ultrasound and a history of meconium in the amniotic fluid. However, the prenatal screen and polyhydramnios are more classically associated with duodenal atresia. Pyloric stenosis presents with projectile vomiting, not bilious vomiting, and typically occurs in the first few weeks of life, not with failure to pass meconium. Necrotizing enterocolitis is a necrotizing disease of the bowel, typically seen in preterm infants, and is not associated with failure to pass meconium or polyhydramnios. Therefore, the most likely etiology, given the clinical presentation, prenatal findings (polyhydramnios, abnormal screen), and failure to pass meconium, is duodenal atresia.", "answer_choice": "A"}
